parts.title: The Case Begins
parts.text: Carmen sat nervously as the judge read the **indictment** against Riverside Factory for dumping waste into the river. As the **plaintiff**, Carmen's family had filed the complaint because pollution had ruined their farm. Her lawyer, Dayo, was ready to **prosecute** the factory owners and hold them accountable. He had studied every **statute** related to water pollution.

parts.title: The Trial Unfolds
parts.text: The factory's lawyer argued that the court lacked **jurisdiction** because the river crossed into another county. But Judge Iris ruled that a local **ordinance** clearly covered the town's waterways. Dayo called a witness who was a well-known **advocate** for clean rivers. The factory's star witness stumbled during questioning, and Dayo suspected **perjury** — the witness may have lied under oath.

parts.title: The Verdict
parts.text: The jury refused to **acquit** the factory — they found it guilty. The judge explained that the factory held full **liability** for damage to the river and surrounding farms. Carmen's family asked for **equity** — not revenge, only fair treatment and a clean river. The factory owners requested **clemency**, hoping for a lighter punishment, but the judge said the fine must match the harm.

parts.title: A New Beginning
parts.text: After the trial, Carmen stood outside the courthouse and took a deep breath. The river would finally be cleaned up, and the farms could begin to recover. Dayo shook her hand and said her courage had made a real difference for the whole town. Carmen smiled, knowing that standing up for what was right had been worth every difficult moment.

definitions.meaning1: A formal accusation that someone has committed a crime. Example: The grand jury issued an indictment against the company for breaking safety rules.
definitions.meaning2: Something that shows a system or situation is bad. Example: The crumbling school building was an indictment of years of neglect.
definitions.studentChallenge: The ___ was read aloud in court, listing all the charges against the accused.

definitions.meaning1: Fairness and justice in the way people are treated. Example: The new rules were designed to promote equity so that every student had an equal chance.
definitions.studentChallenge: The principal believed in ___ and made sure every classroom had the same resources.

definitions.meaning1: Mercy or leniency shown toward someone being punished. Example: The prisoner asked the governor for clemency, hoping for a shorter sentence.
definitions.studentChallenge: The judge showed ___ by giving a lighter punishment to the first-time offender.

definitions.meaning1: To officially charge someone with a crime and bring them to trial. Example: The district attorney decided to prosecute the case herself.
definitions.studentChallenge: The state decided to ___ the company for illegally dumping chemicals.

definitions.meaning1: The crime of lying after promising to tell the truth in court. Example: The witness was charged with perjury after the video proved her story was false.
definitions.studentChallenge: Telling a lie under oath in court is a serious crime called ___.

definitions.meaning1: A person who publicly supports a cause or policy. Example: She became an advocate for animal rights after volunteering at the shelter.
definitions.meaning2: To publicly recommend or support something. Example: The teacher would advocate for longer recess time at every staff meeting.
definitions.studentChallenge: As an ___ for healthy eating, he encouraged the school to serve more fruits and vegetables.

definitions.meaning1: To officially declare that someone is not guilty of a crime. Example: The jury voted to acquit the defendant after reviewing all the evidence.
definitions.studentChallenge: After hearing both sides, the jury decided to ___ the accused of all charges.

definitions.meaning1: The official power to make legal decisions or the area where that power applies. Example: The local court did not have jurisdiction over crimes committed in another state.
definitions.studentChallenge: The case was moved because it fell outside the court's ___.

definitions.meaning1: A person who brings a case against someone else in court. Example: The plaintiff claimed the store had sold him a broken television and refused a refund.
definitions.studentChallenge: The ___ stood before the judge and explained how she had been wronged.

definitions.meaning1: An official rule or law made by a city or town government. Example: A new ordinance required all dogs to be on leashes in the park.
definitions.studentChallenge: The town passed an ___ that banned fireworks after ten o'clock at night.

definitions.meaning1: Legal responsibility for something, especially for paying costs or damages. Example: The company accepted liability for the oil spill and agreed to pay for the cleanup.
definitions.meaning2: Something that causes a disadvantage. Example: His fear of public speaking was a liability in the debate competition.
definitions.studentChallenge: The factory was forced to accept ___ for the pollution it had caused.

definitions.meaning1: A written law passed by a government body. Example: The new statute made it illegal to text while driving in the state.
definitions.studentChallenge: According to the ___, all buildings must have working smoke detectors.

doubleTakeQuiz.sentence1: The grand jury issued a formal _______ against the company for violating safety codes.
doubleTakeQuiz.sentence2: The crumbling roads were an _______ of the city's failure to invest in repairs.

doubleTakeQuiz.sentence1: The new school policy was designed to promote _______ so every student received fair treatment.
doubleTakeQuiz.sentence2: The community group fought for _______ in housing, demanding equal access for all families.

doubleTakeQuiz.sentence1: The governor granted _______ and reduced the prisoner's sentence by five years.
doubleTakeQuiz.sentence2: The defendant's family wrote letters begging the judge for _______, asking for mercy.

doubleTakeQuiz.sentence1: The district attorney vowed to _______ anyone caught dumping waste in the river.
doubleTakeQuiz.sentence2: The government decided to _______ the case after new evidence was discovered.

doubleTakeQuiz.sentence1: The witness was charged with _______ after security footage showed she had lied under oath.
doubleTakeQuiz.sentence2: Committing _______ in a courtroom can result in heavy fines and time in jail.

doubleTakeQuiz.sentence1: The lawyer became a strong _______ for children's rights in the community.
doubleTakeQuiz.sentence2: Several parents gathered to _______ for safer crosswalks near the school.

doubleTakeQuiz.sentence1: After reviewing all the evidence, the jury decided to _______ the defendant of every charge.
doubleTakeQuiz.sentence2: The judge instructed the jury that they must _______ anyone who is not proven guilty beyond a reasonable doubt.

doubleTakeQuiz.sentence1: The local court did not have _______ over crimes that happened across the state line.
doubleTakeQuiz.sentence2: The case was transferred because it fell within federal _______, not the city court's authority.

doubleTakeQuiz.sentence1: The _______ told the jury how the broken sidewalk had caused her to fall and break her arm.
doubleTakeQuiz.sentence2: As the _______ in the lawsuit, Mr. Torres had to prove that the store was responsible for his injury.

doubleTakeQuiz.sentence1: The town council passed a new _______ limiting how late construction noise could continue.
doubleTakeQuiz.sentence2: A local _______ required all restaurants to post their health inspection scores in the window.

doubleTakeQuiz.sentence1: The company accepted full _______ for the accident and paid for all the medical bills.
doubleTakeQuiz.sentence2: His temper was a real _______ during group projects because it made teamwork difficult.

doubleTakeQuiz.sentence1: A new _______ made it illegal to use a phone while driving anywhere in the state.
doubleTakeQuiz.sentence2: The lawyer found an old _______ that supported her client's right to fish in the public lake.

secretPassage.instructions: Read the passage below. Fill in each blank with the correct vocabulary word from the word bank.
secretPassage.passage:
The courtroom buzzed with tension as the _(1)_ stepped forward to tell her story. Mrs. Delgado claimed that the Greenleaf Paper Mill had been dumping chemicals into the town's creek, violating a _(2)_ that protected local waterways. Her lawyer presented the formal _(3)_ listing the charges against the mill.

The mill's attorney argued that the county court had no _(4)_ because the creek flowed through two counties. But the judge pointed to a town _(5)_ that gave the court authority over all water sources within its borders. A well-known environmental _(6)_ took the stand and explained how the chemicals had harmed fish and wildlife.

During cross-examination, one of the mill's witnesses changed his story from what he had said earlier. The judge warned him that _(7)_ was a serious offense. The state was determined to _(8)_ the mill to the fullest extent of the law.

In the end, the jury refused to _(9)_ the mill. They found it guilty and declared it held full _(10)_ for the damage. Mrs. Delgado said she was not seeking revenge — she only wanted _(11)_ and a fair outcome for her community. The mill's owner asked for _(12)_, but the judge insisted the punishment must fit the crime.

wordScaleImposter.studentPick: Pick 2 imposters and explain why they are opposites of the vocabulary word.
